1: A revelation of God: “Hear what the LORD says: Arise, plead your case before the mountains.” The covenant lawsuit (rib) — YHWH calling Israel to legal trial before the eternal witnesses of the mountains. The God who enters legal proceedings with his own people reveals a God who submits to rational accountability, not arbitrary power: “Come now, let us reason together” (Isaiah 1:18). Christ invites the same rationality: “Which one of you convicts me of sin?” (John 8:46); “If what I said is wrong, bear witness about the wrong; but if what I said is right, why do you strike me?” (John 18:23). The God who reasons with his people in Micah is the God who reasons with his accusers in the Gospels.
2: A revelation of God: “Hear, O mountains, the LORD's indictment... for the LORD has an indictment against his people, and he will contend with Israel.” The everlasting foundations of the earth are called as witnesses to YHWH's case against Israel — the most ancient and permanent aspects of creation testify to covenant violation. The NT's form: “the heavens and earth he has kept until the day of judgment and destruction of the ungodly” (2 Peter 3:7). Creation witnesses against human sin; only Christ's righteousness credited to those who believe silences the indictment.
3: A revelation of God: “O my people, what have I done to you? In what have I wearied you? Testify against me.” The most plaintive verse in Micah — YHWH's direct appeal to his people: if you are leaving me, tell me what I have done wrong. This is the language of covenant love grieved by rejection. The NT's fulfillment: Christ's final question to Peter (“Simon, son of John, do you love me?”, John 21:17) and his lament over Jerusalem (“How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings, and you were not willing!”, Matthew 23:37) are the incarnate forms of YHWH's plea in Micah. The God who pleads in Micah 6:3 suffers in Matt 27.
4: A revelation of God: “For I brought you up from the land of Egypt and redeemed you from the house of slavery; and I sent before you Moses, Aaron, and Miriam.” YHWH's recitation of his salvific acts — the Exodus as the ground of the covenant relationship. The God who recounts what he has done for Israel is the God who in Christ says “it is finished” (John 19:30) — the complete salvific act accomplished. The Exodus recital is the OT pattern; the passion narrative is the NT fulfillment of the same pattern: redemption from slavery, leadership through the wilderness, entrance into rest (Heb 3-4). Christ is the new Moses, Aaron (the High Priest, Hebrews 4:14-5:10), and the way (John 14:6).
5: A revelation of God: “Remember what Balak king of Moab devised, and what Balaam the son of Beor answered him... that you may know the saving acts of the LORD.” The Balaam episode — where a hired enemy-prophet was compelled to bless instead of curse — is cited as evidence of YHWH's saving faithfulness. The saving acts of YHWH that Micah recalls are the basis for trust. The NT's fullest “saving act” recitation is in Paul's preaching (Acts 13:17-41: a Micah-style rehearsal of salvific history that culminates in the resurrection). The God who turned Balaam's curse into blessing turns the curse of the cross into the blessing of justification (Galatians 3:13-14).
6: A theme: “With what shall I come before the LORD and bow myself before God on high? Shall I come before him with burnt offerings, with calves a year old?” The question of acceptable worship — what does YHWH actually require? The escalating series of potential offerings (burnt offerings, calves, rams, rivers of oil, firstborn children) builds to the reductio ad absurdum: no sacrifice, however costly, answers the question. The NT's answer: “since we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus” (Hebrews 10:19) — the one sacrifice that actually suffices is not a human offering but the self-offering of Christ. The question Micah's worshipper asks, Christ alone answers.
7: A shadow: “Will the LORD be pleased with thousands of rams, with ten thousands of rivers of oil? Shall I give my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my body for the sin of my soul?” The impossible arithmetic of sacrifice: even infinite rams, infinite oil, even the child's life — none of it reaches the debt. But the question about the firstborn for transgression has a precise answer in the NT: “God did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all” (Romans 8:32). The firstborn that Micah's worshipper considers giving is given — but God gives his own firstborn Son (Colossians 1:15: “the firstborn over all creation”). The unanswerable question finds its answer in the cross.
8: A shadow: “He has told you, O man, what is good; and what does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love kindness (chesed), and to walk humbly with your God?” The ethical summary of the OT that Jesus himself invokes — Jesus in Matthew 23:23 calls “justice and mercy and faithfulness” the “weightier matters of the law” that the Pharisees neglect. Christ is the living fulfillment of Micah 6:8: he does perfect justice (Acts 3:14: “the Holy and Righteous One”), loves perfect chesed (John 13:1: “having loved his own who were in the world, he loved them to the end”), and walks in perfect humility (Philippians 2:8: “humbled himself by becoming obedient to the point of death”). What YHWH requires, Christ embodies; what Christ embodies, he works in those who trust him.
9: A revelation of God: “The voice of the LORD cries to the city: wisdom shall fear your name.” YHWH speaks to the city; wisdom is defined by fearing his name. The God who speaks is the God whose word is wisdom. The NT identifies Christ as the wisdom of God: “Christ, in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3); “Christ the power of God and the wisdom of God” (1 Corinthians 1:24). To fear the name of YHWH is, in the NT, to fear and reverence the name of Jesus, in whom YHWH's name is fully revealed (John 17:6: “I have manifested your name to the people whom you gave me”).
10: A revelation of God: “Can I forget any longer the treasures of wickedness in the house of the wicked, and the scant measure that is accursed?” YHWH's omniscient awareness of the hidden dishonest scales — “can I forget?” The God who never forgets the weights and measures of injustice is the God before whom “no creature is hidden, and all are naked and exposed” (Hebrews 4:13). Christ's final judgment includes everything hidden: “God will bring every deed into judgment, with every secret thing, whether good or evil” (Ecclesiastes 12:14; cf. Matthew 12:36: “for every careless word... you will give account”). The scales Micah's merchants manipulate, Christ weighs with perfect precision.
11: A revelation of God: “Shall I acquit the man with wicked scales and with a bag of deceitful weights?” — the rhetorical answer is no. YHWH will not acquit the guilty. This is the problem of justice that the gospel addresses: how can YHWH both “not acquit the guilty” (Exodus 34:7) and yet justify sinners (Romans 4:5)? The answer is the cross: “God presented Christ as a sacrifice of atonement... he did it to demonstrate his righteousness at the present time, so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus” (Romans 3:25-26). YHWH does not acquit — he condemns, but in Christ, the condemnation falls on the one who bore it for us.
12: A theme: “Your wealthy men are full of violence; your inhabitants speak lies, and their tongue is deceitful in their mouth.” Wealth, violence, and deception as a unified syndrome of covenant breakdown. James 5:1-6 echoes this exactly against those who have withheld wages; Revelation 18:11-13 catalogues the merchants of Babylon whose wealth is built on exploitation. Christ's “blessed are the poor in spirit” (Matthew 5:3) and “woe to you who are rich” (Luke 6:24) are the NT forms of Micah's indictment of wealthy violence.
13: A shadow: “Therefore I strike you with a grievous blow, making you desolate because of your sins.” The divine blow of covenant judgment. The NT uses the same image for Christ: “it was the will of the LORD to crush him; he has put him to grief” (Isaiah 53:10). The grievous blow that falls on Micah's sinful community falls, in the NT, on the one who bore the sins of the community — so that the blow that is deserved by many falls on the one who deserves it not at all. The desolation Micah threatens is what Christ endures on the cross (“My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?”, Matthew 27:46) in order to prevent the permanent desolation of those who trust him.
14: A shadow: “You shall eat, but not be satisfied, and there shall be hunger within you.” The curse of hunger despite eating — the covenant curse that reverses the blessing of satisfied feasting (Deuteronomy 28:47: “because you did not serve the LORD your God with joyfulness and gladness of heart”). Christ is the reversal of this curse: “I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst” (John 6:35). The hunger that the covenant curse installs, Christ removes — not by providing food but by being himself the food that permanently satisfies.
15: A theme: “You shall sow but not reap; you shall tread olives but not anoint yourselves with oil.” The covenant curse of futile labor — work that produces nothing usable. The reversal of the creation mandate in which labor yields fruit. The NT's form: “apart from me you can do nothing” (John 15:5) — without connection to the vine, all labor is futile. Connected to Christ, labor becomes fruitful: “I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit” (John 15:5). The fruitlessness of the covenant curse is reversed in the fruitfulness of the new covenant vine.
16: A shadow: “For you have kept the statutes of Omri, and all the works of the house of Ahab; and you have walked in their counsels, that I may make you a desolation, and your inhabitants a hissing.” Omri and Ahab — the two kings most associated with syncretism, Baal worship, and social injustice in the northern kingdom — become the pattern that Judah has imitated. Following the worst precedents always leads to the worst outcomes. The NT equivalent is Paul's catalog of bad examples in 1 Corinthians 10:1-11: “these things took place as examples for us, that we might not desire evil as they did.” Christ is the positive example that replaces every negative one: “I have given you an example, that you also should do just as I have done to you” (John 13:15).
